text.fullText:
The following is a response to the petitions calling for rulemaking changes for transparency in property broker transactions.
I have been in business for 30 years and fear this rulemaking if passed would lead to the end of this small business that supports ten families and many like me.
Imagine this: car dealers are forced to send to you what they paid for the car they sold you 48 hours after you bought it. You have that information, and your cousin Vinny is going to buy the same vehicle, you let Vinny know. Vinny now has an upper hand to negotiate because he knows what the dealer’s margin is. This is not how the free market works and this is what OOIDA is looking for. If this regulation were to be pushed through it would not only significantly impair brokers but would negatively affect the markets and all its players as a whole.
In a free market prices are being driven up and down by supply and demand. It is the responsibility of shippers, brokers, and carriers a like to follow the free market and understand where the equilibrium of the market is at and adjust your service prices accordingly. Brokers are offering freight to the motor carriers for a specified rate and carriers have the option to think freely for themselves on if they should accept the tender or reject. No one is forcing them to take a load.
The effect of such a change will create a negative shockwave that will be irreversible throughout the transportation industry. This regulation will only impair the transportation industry from operating as it should under free market processes. Carriers compete with brokers as well as needing them to fill lanes that they don’t have direct shippers in. They have every opportunity to set their own destinies and margins when negotiating with brokers on a spot load basis. If carriers aren’t happy with what they are being paid then they do not take the load. If we as brokers are required to show our margins-what purpose will this accomplish other than give the carriers the upper hand in future negotiations as well as open the door for carriers to give the rates to our competition or try to go directly with customers themselves(backdooring). Our rates with customers are confidential agreements with proprietary information between us and our customers. Customers look to us to assure the information doesn’t get in the hands of their competition.

Brokers spend countless dollars and hours of effort to seek out, secure, and keep customers. It is unreasonable and unfair to think that the by forcing brokers to release how much their customer is paying them to the carriers is a fair or accurate representation of how much the brokerage acutely profits. Gross margins on a load-to-load basis is not reflective of the net profit that brokers receive. By adding this unneeded regulation it will only add additional cost to the ever growing list of expenses. To achieve what OOIDA wants it will require brokers and motor carriers alike to integrate with additional software which costs anywhere from $2,500-$10,000 per integration. If carries are so enthralled with brokerages and their margins they should consider putting the same time and effort into getting and buying a sales team, a dispatch team, buy the capital assets required, TMS, insurances, wages, health care, 401(k), bonuses, etc and they would get to see what their margins anytime they would like since they so deeply care about a percentage in which does not take into account of all the expense that occur in a transaction.
Some concerns if the proposal is enacted are:
•Carriers will back solicit the shippers because they know the rate and undercut the brokers to directly obtain the freight. This will set up a huge number of lawsuits that will harm brokers and shippers as well as carriers involved.
•Brokers go out of business because the business model is no longer sustainable
•Small Carriers run out of options for backhauls, shippers turn to their only reliable option, the largest carriers, Owner Operators and small fleets must align themselves with a large carriers and find themselves in a worse position than before the petition
In simple terms regulation drives consolidation. Small companies like ours will be the most adversely effected. We have seen it occur in other industries and OOIDA and its members will not be no different.
We are requesting that the FMCSA does not allow this anti-free market, ill-logical, pro-government regulation, to go any further than it has. We appreciate your consideration in the matter and look forward to a positive ruling.
Thank you for your consideration.

Best regards
